source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Twelve weights have integer masses of $1 \mathrm{~g}, 2 \mathrm{~g}, 3 \mathrm{~g}, \ldots, 11 \mathrm{~g}$ and $12 \mathrm{~g}$ respectively. A vendor divides those weights up into 3 groups of 4 weights each. The total mass of the first group is $41 \mathrm{~g}$, the mass of the second group is $26 \mathrm{~g}$ (see diagram). Which of the following weights is in the same group as the weight with $9 \mathrm{~g}$?
<image1>
extraction_notes: options field is an unlabeled array; mapped by order to A-E

issues: Top-level image_paths and metadata.image_paths are inconsistent: 'outputs/mathvision_300_3040/run_42c44b29896259fd/datasets/mathvision/artifacts/images/prob_b90b9d955ccd5b9034617591_primary.png' vs 'images/1479.jpg'.
choice_count=5 and metadata.choice_field='options', but no options field/value is provided in the input.

image_dependency: The grouping information appears to depend on a visual arrangement of weights, not text alone
multi_step: Solving requires combining the fixed set of masses 1g through 12g with the given group sums 41g and 26g
The task asks which weight is in the same group as 9g, implying inference from both diagram placement and arithmetic constraints
This is more than direct lookup; it needs multi-condition elimination or partition reasoning
verifiability: answer_type is short_text with a specific numeric mass ('$7 \mathrm{~g}$')
The problem is multiple-choice with 5 explicit options, so the final answer is easy to check
The underlying reasoning is constrained by exact integer masses and exact group sums, which makes the result objectively checkable
